section_name_raw: During the financial year, 15,00,000 shares were approved to be offered under Sona Performance based Share Plan (PSP -2025) by the
content_markdown:
# During the financial year, 15,00,000 shares were approved to be offered under Sona Performance based Share Plan (PSP -2025) by the 
shareholders on 19th December, 2025 over a period of 5 (five) years based on the performance criteria specified thereunder and out of which 
1,25,915 shares were allotted in Financial Year 2025-26 to Mr. Vivek Vikram Singh. He was earlier granted 1,103,400 options under the ESOP 
– 2023 and during the financial year he did not exercise any options under the ESOP – 2023. There is an increase of 15 % in his remuneration 
and addition of annual commission i.e. 1% of consolidated PAT and his total remuneration capped at 5% of the net profit of the Company.
	
^ Mr. Vikram Verma Vadapalli was granted 7,02,000 options under the ESOP - 2023. During the financial year, he did not exercise any options 
under the ESOP – 2023. Mr. Verma was appointed as whole time Director on 24th January, 2025.
	
@ Mr. Rohit Nanda was granted 562,000 options under the ESOP - 2023. During the financial year, he did not exercise any options under the 
ESOP – 2023.
	
* Mr. Ajay Pratap Singh was granted 2,14,800 options under the ESOP - 2023. During the financial year, Mr. Ajay has exercised 3,000 options 
under the ESOP – 2023 and there is no perquisite value of it.
2.	
Remuneration paid to the Non-Executive Directors for 
the Financial Year ended on 31st March, 2026
	
The Shareholders of the Company at the AGM held on 
9th September, 2021, have approved the payment of 
remuneration to be paid to the Non-Executive Directors 
(including independent directors) not exceeding 3% of 
the net profits of the Company, calculated in accordance 
with Section 198 of the Act, in a manner as decided 
by the Board of Directors subject to upper limit of INR 
70,000,000 (Indian rupees seventy million) for each 
financial year for a period of 5 financial year upto Financial 
Year 2025-26.
	
The Company understands and acknowledges the 
importance of remunerating well to its Independent 
Directors and Non-Executive and Non-independent 
Directors of the Company to get optimum benefit of their 
wisdom, experience and expertise for the benefit of all 
the stakeholders of the Company.
	
Considering the growth in the Net Profit of the Company, 
as calculated under section 198 of the Companies Act, 
2013, over the period of time since 2021, the Board of 
Directors in its meeting held on 20th March, 2026 and 
subject to approval of shareholders in the ensuing 
Annual General Meeting of the Company, has approved 
and revised the total limit of commission i.e. upto 1% Net 
Profit of the Company calculated in terms of section 198 
of the Companies Act, 2013 and with an overall upper 
cap of INR 70,000,000 (Indian rupees seventy million) for 
each financial year starting from 2026-27 for five financial 
years, to be payable to all the Non-Executive Directors 
(including Independent Directors and Non-Independent 
Directors) of the Company.
	
The Board also considered that since a reasonable 
time has been elapsed and considering the dynamic 
regulatory landscape, ever rising bar of Corporate 
Governance and increasing responsibilities demanding 
more and more involvement of non-executive directors 
and in particular the role being played by the Chairperson 
of the Board and Audit Committee, it is reasonable to 
revise the commission payable to Independent Directors 
and Non-Executive and Non-Independent Director of 
the Company.
	
The Board of Directors in its meeting held on 20th March, 2026 also approved the revision in the commission payable to 
Independent Directors and Non-Executive and Non- Independent Director of the Company, effective from Financial Year 
2026-27 for a period of 5 (five) financial years subject to overall cap of INR 70,000,000 (Indian rupees seventy million) as 
follows:

During the year under review, the Company did not 
grant any loans to any of its directors. Further, except 
as stated above, there are no pecuniary relationships 
or transactions between the Independent Directors 
and the Company, other than the sitting fees and 
commission paid to the Non-Executive and Independent 
Directors. Further, the criteria for making payment to non-
executive Directors has been disclosed on the website 
of the Company at https://sonacomstar.com/files/policy/
nomination-and-remuneration-policy-policy-2ORHuA.
pdf.
	
Further, no notice period and severance fee are payable 
to directors of the Company as on 31st March, 2026. The 
Company has not granted any stock option to its Non-
Executive Directors.
	
ROLE OF COMPANY SECRETARY IN OVERALL 
GOVERNANCE PROCESS
	
The Company Secretary plays a key role in ensuring that 
the Board (including committees thereof) procedures 
are followed and regularly reviewed. The Company 
Secretary ensures that all relevant information, details 
and documents are made available to the Directors 
and Senior Management for effective decision-making 
at the meetings. The Company Secretary is primarily 
responsible to assist and advice the Board in conduct 
of affairs of the Company, to ensure compliance with 
applicable statutory requirements and Secretarial 
Standards, to provide guidance to directors and to 
facilitate convening of meetings and to carry out such 
other functions as may be prescribed in different Acts, 
Rules and Regulations. He act as an interface between the 
management and regulatory authorities for governance 
matters.
	
CEO & CFO CERTIFICATION
	
The Managing Director & Group CEO and the Group Chief 
Financial Officer of the Company are required to provide 
a compliance certificate to the board of directors in terms 
of Regulation 17(8) of Listing Regulations, copy of which 
was placed before the Board of Directors in their meeting 
held on 30th April, 2026 and is annexed as Annexure – III 
of this Report.
	
MEANS OF COMMUNICATION
	
The Company recognises the importance of communication with Shareholders and promptly discloses information on 
material corporate developments and other events as required under the Listing Regulations. Such timely disclosures 
indicate the good corporate governance practices of the Company. 
Publication of Financials 
Results
Quarterly, half-yearly and annual financial results are published in ‘Business Standard’ in English 
language (all editions) and in Hindi language (Delhi edition) along with the QR code of link of the full 
financial results along with the auditor report uploaded on the website of the Company.
Further, the Company as a good corporate governance practice sends out the financial results of 
every quarter to its shareholders, whose email-Ids are registered with their Depository Participant/ 
Registrar and Share Transfer Agent (RTA) of the Company.
During the financial year, the unaudited quarterly results were announced within the required time of 
45 (forty-five) days of the closure of each quarter. The audited annual results were announced within 
30 days from the end of the financial year against the requirement to announced within 60 (sixty) days 
from the end of the financial year as required under the Listing Regulations. 
The aforesaid financial results are announced to the Stock Exchanges within the statutory time period 
from the conclusion of the Board Meeting(s) at which the results are considered and approved.
Website
The Company’s website www.sonacomstar.com contains a dedicated segment called ‘Investors’ 
where all information needed by members is available. Further, as per the requirement of National 
Stock Exchange of India Limited and BSE Limited, the Company has a separate sub tab under Investor 
i.e. “Disclosure under Regulations 46 of Listing Regulations”, where the Company have disclosed 
information required under Regulations 46 of the Listing Regulations.

The website, inter-alia, also displays information regarding schedule of analyst or institutional investor 
meet, presentation made to media/ analysts/ institutional investors, transcript & recording of Earnings 
Calls, press releases, Investors Manual which includes Investor’s Charter and FAQs, shareholder’s 
satisfaction survey, Sustainability Report, CSR details, stock information, shareholding patterns, 
quarterly corporate governance reports, details of unclaimed dividend, etc.
Press Release
The Company has released the press release for each of the quarterly financial results of the Company 
and all other corporate developments and uploaded the same on the website of stock exchanges and 
on its website at https://sonacomstar.com/investor/investor-presentations.
BSE Listing Centre & NEAPS 
(NSE Electronic Application 
Processing System)
All disclosures and communications to BSE Limited (BSE) and National Stock Exchange of India 
Limited (NSE) are filed electronically through BSE Listing Centre and NEAPS.
Annual Report and AGM 
Notices
In our endeavour to protect the environment, the Company had sent the Annual Report and AGM 
notices for the year 2024-2025 through e-mails to those members who had registered their e-mail 
ids with either the Company or with depository.
The Company also sent the letter providing the web-link, including the exact path, where complete 
details of the Annual Report and AGM Notice are available to those shareholder(s), who have not so 
registered their email Id with the Company or with depository.
The Company also sends the Hard copy of the full annual report to those shareholders, who request 
for the same at the email id investor@sonacomstar.com. 
Exclusive e-mail id’s for 
investors
Following e-mail ids have been exclusively dedicated for the investors’ queries: investor@sonacomstar.
com and einward.ris@kfintech.com 
Investor's Charter and FAQs for 
the Shareholders
The Investor Relations page of the Company’s website provides Investors Manual which contains both 
Investor’s Charter specifying the rights of the investors under various applicable laws and protection 
of minority shareholders from oppression and mis-management and Frequently Asked Questions 
on various topics related to transfers, transmissions and transportation of shares, dematerialisation/
rematerialisation, KYC updation, nomination, change of address, loss of share certificates. In addition, 
various downloadable forms required to be executed by the Shareholders have also been provided 
on the website of the Company viz. https://sonacomstar.com/investor-relations.
Shareholders Satisfaction 
Survey
The Company voluntarily placed shareholders survey page on its website to facilitate the engagement 
of Shareholders and is an endeavour to improve shareholders services which can be assessed at 
https://sonacomstar.com/shareholders-survey.
Reminder emails
Quaterly reminders are, inter alia, sent to shareholders for registering their PAN, KYC & nomination 
detail, bank details and unclaimed dividend thereto.
Investor Presentation
The presentations on performance of the Company are placed on the Company’s website before 
investor call/meeting, for all the stakeholders.
During the financial year under review, the Company conducted quarterly earnings calls/meetings 
with investors immediately after declaration of financial results to brief them on the performance of 
the Company and on an average the company spent approx. 60 minutes per call. The Company also 
uploads the audio recordings and transcript of such calls on the website of the Company at https://
sonacomstar.com/investor/investor-presentations.
Green Initiative
All agenda papers for the Board and Committee meetings are disseminated electronically through 
e-mail to the directors and invitees.
	
GENERAL SHAREHODLER’S INFORMATION
1.
Date, time and venue of the 
Annual General Meeting
Wednesday, 15th July, 2026 at 12:00 Noon (IST) through video conferencing (VC) / other 
audio-visual means (OAVM).
2.
Financial Year
1st April, 2025 to 31st March, 2026.
3.
Dividend payment
Interim Dividend:
The Board of Directors in their meeting held on 23rd January,  2026 had declared an interim 
dividend of INR 1.60 (One rupee and sixty paisa only) per equity share of the Company 
having face value of INR 10 (Indian rupees ten only) each to only those shareholders whose 
names appear in the register of members / beneficial owners on 30th January,  2026 and the 
dividend was paid on 12th February, 2026 directly to their bank accounts.
Final Dividend:
The Board of Directors at their meeting held on 30th  April, 2026 has recommended payment 
of INR 1.80 (One Indian Rupee and eighty paisa) per Equity Share of the Company having 
face value of INR 10/- (Indian rupees ten only) each as the final dividend for the Financial 
Year ended 31st March, 2026 to the shareholders of the Company. The final dividend will be 
paid only to those shareholders whose names appear in the register of members/beneficial 
owners on Friday, 26th June, 2026. The payment of final dividend is subject to the approval of 
the shareholders in the ensuing Annual General Meeting of the Company.

queries etc.

16.	 Disclosure on foreign exchange risk and hedging 
activities. 
	
Your Company operates internationally, and a large 
portion of the business is transacted in international 
currencies primarily in US$ and EUR. Consequently, 
we are exposed to foreign exchange risk through 
our sales and services in the USA, Europe, China and 
other countries across the world, and purchases from 
overseas suppliers in various foreign currencies. The 
Company carries out currency hedging based upon a 
Board approved hedging policy which primarily employs 
forward contracts as a medium of hedging its currency 
risk.

(eight) are located in India.

19.	 List of all credit ratings obtained by the Company along 
with any revisions thereto, for all debt instruments 
of the Company or any fixed deposit programme or 
any scheme or proposal of the Company involving 
mobilisation of funds, whether in India or abroad
	
The credit rating of the Company can be accessed at 
https://sonacomstar.com/investor/credit-rating.
	
Other Disclosures
	
The Company has complied with the requirements 
specified in Regulations 17 to 27 and Clauses (b) to 
(i) of sub-regulation (2) of Regulation 46 of the Listing 
Regulations.
	
Related Party Transaction
	
All transactions entered into with related parties as 
defined under the Act and Regulation 23 of the SEBI 
Listing Regulations, as amended, during the year under 
review undertaken an arm’s length pricing ('ALP') and 
basis and in the ordinary course of business ('OCB'). 
These have been approved by the Audit Committee. 
Certain transactions which were repetitive in nature 
were approved by way of omnibus approval by the Audit 
Committee.
	
The Audit Committee takes into consideration the 
management representation and an Independent 
Consultant’s Report, wherever required, whilst 
scrutinising and approving the related party transactions, 
from the perspective of fulfilling the criteria of meeting 
arm's length basis and OCB.
	
The Audit Committee reviews on a quarterly basis, the 
details of related party transactions, entered into by the 
Company pursuant to the omnibus approval granted.
	
The Company did not enter into any material related 
party transactions during the Financial Year 2025-26. 
The details of the related party transactions are set out 
in the note no. 36 to standalone financial statements 
forming part of this Annual Report. Further, the related 
party transactions undertaken by the Company were in 
compliance with the provisions set out in the Act and the 
Listing Regulations.
	
During the year, no materially significant transaction 
was entered into by the Company with its related parties 
that may have a potential conflict with the interest of 
the Company.
	
In addition to the above and as required under the Listing 
Regulations, the Company is in compliance with the 
Accounting Standards on related party disclosures and 
submitting disclosures of related party transactions to the 
Stock Exchanges in the prescribed format on half yearly 
basis and also publishing the same on its website at 
https://sonacomstar.com/investor/financial-information.
	
During the financial year under review, there was no 
transaction of a material nature with any of the related 
parties, which was in conflict with the interests of the 
Company.
	
During the year under review, the Policy on Related Party 
Transactions was amended to affect the amendments 
brought in the Listing Regulations, by SEBI vide its 
notification dated November 18, 2025. The Policy 
is disclosed on the Company’s website at https://
sonacomstar.com/files/policy/policy-on-related-party-
transactions-policy-rQXZb0.pdf.
	
Details of Non-Compliance
	
No penalties or strictures were imposed on the Company 
by Stock Exchanges or SEBI or any other statutory 
authority on any matter related to the capital market, 
during the last three years, except as follows:
	
The National Stock Exchange of India Limited and BSE 
Limited levied a penalty of INR 46,000/- plus gst each 
on the Company for non- compliance of Regulation 
19(1)/19(2) of the Listing Regulations, i.e. Chairperson 
of the Nomination and Remuneration Committee 
(NRC) was appointed as Chairperson of the Board 
w.e.f. 23rd June, 2025 after the sudden demise of Late 
Mr. Sunjay Kapur, Former Chairman and Non-Executive 
Director and the same was rectified on 16th July, 2025 by 
appointing Mr. Karamendra Daulet Singh, as Chairperson 
of NRC. The respective fine amounts were paid to both 
National Stock Exchange of India Limited and BSE 
Limited.
	
Your Company has complied with requirements of 
regulatory authorities.
	
Vigil Mechanism/Whistle Blower Policy
	
In accordance with the provisions of the Act and 
Regulation 22 of the Listing Regulations, the Company has 
in place a Vigil Mechanism and a Whistle-Blower Policy 
duly approved by the Audit Committee which provides a 
formal mechanism for all Directors and employees of the 
Company to approach the Management of the Company 
(Audit Committee in case where the concern involves the 
Senior Management) and make protective disclosures 
to the Management about unethical behaviour, actual or 
suspected fraud or violation of the Company’s Code of 
Conduct or Ethics Policy.
	
The Company has a well-defined Whistle Blower Policy 
and the same is also covered in the Code of Conduct 
and Business Ethics and are fully implemented by the 
management, particulars of which have been explained 
in greater detail as part of the Board’s Report.
	
During the year, no personnel has been denied access to 
the Audit Committee.
	
The Whistle Blower Policy of the Company is available 
on the website of the Company and can be access at 
https://sonacomstar.com/files/policy/whistle-blower-
policy-policy-MUwqe2.pdf. The details about the vigil 
mechanism form part of the Board’s Report.
	
Board Diversity
	
Your Company believes that a truly diverse Board will 
leverage differences in thought, perspective, knowledge, 
skill, regional and industry experience, cultural and 
geographical background, age, race and gender, which 
will ensure that the Company retains its competitive 
advantage. Your Company further believes that a diverse 
Board will contribute towards driving business results, 
make corporate governance more effective, enhance 
quality and responsible decision- making capability, 
ensure sustainable development and enhance the 
reputation of the Company.
	
The NRC has framed a Policy for Board Diversity which 
lays down the criteria for appointment of Directors on 
the Board of your Company and guides organisation’s 
approach to Board Diversity.
	
While recommending the appointment of a director, the 
NRC considers the manner in which the function and 
domain expertise of the individual can contribute to the 
overall skill-domain mix of the Board.
	
The Board of Directors review the policy from time to 
time. The policy on Board Diversity has been placed on 
the Company’s website at https://sonacomstar.com/files/
policy/policy-on-board-diversity-policy-sKCWRQ.pdf.

Artificial Intelligence (AI)/Machine Learning (ML) and 
Operational Technology (OT) Analytics
	
Over the past year, the Company has made significant 
strides in building its AI/ML and OT analytics capabilities 
with a clear focus on operational value and scalability. 
A key milestone was the successful completion of an 
OT data lake pilot, establishing a secure and structured 
foundation for predictive and prescriptive analytics 
across manufacturing operations. Multiple AI/ML 
solutions — including energy forecasting models, OCR-
based automation, self-service analytics dashboards, 
and LLM-powered utilities — were developed and 
deployed enterprise-wide using low-code and open-
source tools, delivering measurable outcomes with 
minimal incremental cost.
	
Standardisation of OT data flows in collaboration with 
manufacturing teams, along with the revival of operational 
dashboards, has strengthened data quality and decision-
making at both the shop floor and management levels. 
In addition to project-specific solutions, the team is also 
building internal self-service exploratory data analytics 
tools — designed as no-code, low-code platforms 
— to empower business users across functions to 
independently analyse data, generate insights, and 
make informed decisions. This initiative goes beyond 
delivering projects; it is aimed at spreading analytics 
literacy and proficiency across the organisation, enabling 
a culture where data-driven decision-making becomes 
an everyday capability rather than a specialised skill.
	
To accelerate the next phase of growth, a scalable, 
secure, cloud-based AI infrastructure has been 
established to empower cross-functional teams to build 
and deploy Generative AI and Machine Learning tools 
at scale — enabling rapid experimentation, faster time-
to-value, and reduced dependency on external vendors 
for AI development. Additionally, a formal Enterprise 
AI Usage Policy (aligned with ISO/IEC 42001 and NIST 
AI RMF) was established, covering AI governance, risk 
assessment, lifecycle management, and responsible 
use — positioning the company for safe, scalable, and 
responsible AI adoption across business units.
	
Disclosure of Commodity Price Risk and Commodity 
Hedging Activities
	
The Company consumes alloy steel, steel, aluminium 
and copper as some of the major commodities directly 
or through procured components for manufacturing 
its products. Majority of this commodity price risk is 
a passthrough with some time lag depending upon 
customer contracts, therefore your Company doesn’t 
specifically undertake commodity hedging separately.

C.	
Changes made in the Policies during the year
	
During the financial year under review, the Company has amended the following policies/codes in accordance with the 
requirements of the applicable laws. The gist of the changes made in the policies are as follows:
S. 
No.
Name of the Policies
Description of the Policy
Summary of key changes made to the Policies during the 
Financial Year
1.
Nomination & 
Remuneration 
Policy
The Policy is intended to set out criteria to pay 
equitable remuneration to the Directors, Key 
Managerial Personnel, Senior Management
Personnel and other employees of the 
Company and to harmonise the aspirations 
of human resources with the goals of the 
Company.
Clause 7.3 of the policy pertaining to the 
remuneration of Managing Director/Executive 
Director/CEO has been revised to broadening 
its scope.
2.
Policy on 
Related Party 
Transactions
This policy has been framed with an intent 
to regulate all Related Party Transactions in 
the Company.
•	
The definition of ‘Material Related Party 
Transaction’ and ‘Retail Purchases’ has 
been amended in accordance with the 
amendment in the Listing Regulations.
•	
The thresholds for transactions where 
subsidiary is a party, but the Company is 
not a party and which requires approval 
of Audit Committee of the Company, has 
been amended in line with the amended 
provisions of the Listing Regulations.
•	
A clause related to validity of the omnibus 
approval granted by shareholders in AGM 
has been duly added.
3.
Code of Conduct 
for Prohibition of 
Insider Trading
The Code is adopted to regulate, monitor 
and report trading by designated persons 
and their immediate relatives and thereby 
putting in place a framework for prohibition 
of insider trading.
•	
The existing definition of Unpublished 
Price Sensitive Information/UPSI has 
been amended in accordance with the 
amendment in the SEBI (PIT) Regulation 
2015, pertaining to the definition of UPSI.
•	
For ensuring alignment with amended 
provisions of the SEBI PIT Regulations, 
proviso(s) were inserted specifying timeline 
for making entry in SDD and requirement of 
trading window closure in case of UPSI not 
emanating within the Company.

FORM NO. MR - 3
SECRETARIAL AUDIT REPORT
FOR THE FINANCIAL YEAR ENDED MARCH 31, 2026
[Pursuant to section 204(1) of the Companies Act, 2013 and Rule 9 of the Companies  
(Appointment and Remuneration of Managerial Personnel) Rules, 2014]
To,
The Members
Sona BLW Precision Forgings Limited
L27300HR1995PLC083037
We have conducted the secretarial audit of the compliance 
of applicable statutory provisions and the adherence to good 
corporate practices by Sona BLW Precision Forgings Limited 
(hereinafter referred to as “the Company”). The Secretarial 
Audit was conducted in a manner that provided us with a 
reasonable basis for evaluating the corporate conduct/
statutory compliances and expressing our opinion thereon for 
the financial year ended on March 31, 2026 (“Audit Period”). 
The Company operate in Mobility components, systems and 
sub-systems. The Company is listed on both National Stock 
Exchange of India Limited and BSE Limited.
Limitation of the Auditors
(i)	
Based on our verification of the Company’s books, 
papers, minute books, forms and returns filed and 
other records maintained by the Company and also the 
information provided by the Company, its officers, agents 
and authorised representatives during the conduct of 
Secretarial Audit, we hereby report that in our opinion, 
the Company has, during the Audit Period, complied with 
the statutory provisions listed hereunder; and
(ii)	
Based on the management representation, confirmation 
and explanation wherever required by us, the Company 
has proper Board-processes and compliance-mechanism 
in place to the extent, in the manner and subject to the 
reporting made hereinafter.
Auditors Responsibility
(i)	
Our responsibility is to express the opinion on compliance 
with the applicable laws and maintenance of records 
based on audit. We conducted our audit in accordance 
with the Guidance Note on Secretarial Audit (“Guidance 
Note”) and Auditing Standards issued by the Institute 
of Company Secretaries of India (“ICSI”). The Guidance 
Note and Auditing Standards require that we comply with 
statutory and regulatory requirements and also that we 
plan and perform the audit so as to obtain reasonable 
assurance about compliance with applicable laws and 
maintenance of records.
(ii)	
Due to the inherent limitations of an audit including 
internal, financial and operating controls, there is an 
unavoidable risk that some misstatements or material 
non-compliances may not be detected, even though the 
audit is properly planned and performed in accordance 
with the Standards.
(iii)	 Our audit involves performing procedures to obtain audit 
evidence about the adequacy of compliance mechanism 
exist in the Company to assess any material weakness 
and testing and evaluating the design and operating 
effectiveness of compliance mechanism based upon the 
assessed risk. The procedures selected depend upon 
the auditor’s judgement, including assessment of the risk 
of material non-compliance whether due to error or fraud.
(iv)	 We believe that the audit evidence we have obtained 
is sufficient and appropriate to provide a basis for our 
audit opinion on the Company’s Board processes and 
compliance mechanism.
We have examined the books, papers, minute books, forms 
and returns filed, and other records made available to us 
and maintained by the Company during the Audit Period, 
according to the provisions of:
(i)	
The Companies Act, 2013 (‘the Act’) and the rules made 
thereunder;
(ii)	
The Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act, 1956 (‘SCRA’) 
and the rules made thereunder;
(iii)	 The Depositories Act, 1996 and the Regulations and Bye-
laws framed thereunder; (Not applicable during the Audit 
Period)
(iv)	 Foreign Exchange Management Act, 1999 and the rules 
and regulations made thereunder to the extent of Foreign 
Direct Investment and Overseas Direct Investment;
(v)	
The following Regulations and Guidelines prescribed 
under the Securities and Exchange Board of India Act, 
1992 (‘SEBI Act’): -
	
a.	
The Securities and Exchange Board of India 
(Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) 
Regulations, 2011;
	
b.	
The Securities and Exchange Board of India 
(Prohibition of Insider Trading) Regulations, 2015;
	
c.	
The Securities and Exchange Board of India (Issue of 
Capital and Disclosure Requirements) Regulations, 
2018; (Not applicable during the Audit Period)
	
d.	
The Securities and Exchange Board of India (Share 
Based Employee Benefits and Sweat Equity) 
Regulations, 2021;
	
e.	
The Securities and Exchange Board of India 
(Issue and Listing of Non-Convertible Securities) 
Regulations, 2021; (Not applicable during the Audit 
Period)
	
f.	
The Securities and Exchange Board of India 
(Registrars to an Issue and Share Transfer Agents) 
Annexure - C
Regulations, 1993 regarding the Companies Act 
and dealing with client;
	
g.	
The Securities and Exchange Board of India

A-	 CONSERVATION OF ENERGY
(i)	
Steps taken or impact on conservation of energy;
	
Energy conservation initiatives by implementing energy 
efficient technologies continues to be the top focus in 
the Company’s operations. The Company believes that 
its actions around energy bring direct benefit greatly to 
the climate and to the Company’s bottom line as well.
	
During the financial year under review, the Company 
was able to achieve energy savings through various 
operations initiatives as under:
	
1.	
Heating Furnace Efficiency Improvement: Applied 
high emissivity coatings and did improvement in 
furnace insulation to minimise heat loss. Optimised 
furnace cycle time. Utilised waste heat from furnace 
flue gases for preheating applications.
	
2.	
Reduction of Idle Energy Consumption in Shot 
Blasting Machines: Installed automatic shut-off 
systems and programmed motors to stop after 10 
minutes of idle condition
	
3.	
Replacement of Electrical Heating with Solar 
Heating in Phosphating Process: Installed of 
solar water heating system to replace electrical 
immersion heaters, reducing the dependency on 
grid electricity
	
4.	
Implementation of Energy Saving Control in 
Equipment: Implemented auto idle shutdown logic 
for activation of energy-saving modes in various 
forging and CNC line equipment, shot pinning and 
shot blasting machines.
	
5.	
Optimisation of Air Compressor Uses in machine 
shop: Enhanced energy efficiency by reduction 
in compressor unloading pressure, air flow 
optimisation, installation of solenoid valve for idle 
condition control and automatic drain valve in air 
receiver tank
	
6.	
Design change in Presses: changed of hydraulic 
design and introduction of lower capacity motors 
in 100 ton and 600 ton presses for energy 
consumption reduction
	
7.	
Installation of Energy Efficient HVAC Systems: 
Replaced conventional cooler fans with energy 
efficient HVLS fans, installed energy efficient VRV 
ACs.
	
8.	
Smart Air Compression/Cooling tower motor 
Systems: The adoption of variable frequency drive 
(VFD)-based air compressors/cooling tower motor 
allows for optimised performance, adjusting to 
demand rather than running at a constant load.
	
9.	
Leakage Identification & Air Compression 
Efficiency: A company-wide drive was launched 
to detect and eliminate compressed air leakages, 
significantly improving the overall efficiency of air 
compressors.
(ii)	
Steps taken by the Company for utilising alternate 
sources of energy;
	
For utilisation of alternate source of power, we harnessed 
16,847 GJ from our roof top solar installations which is 
approx. 4% of our total energy consumption during the 
financial year 2025-26.
	
During the financial year, the Company has executed 
definitive documents with CSE Development (India) 
Private Limited, Cleantech group entity for setting up 
of 2.5 MWp group captive power generating facility at 
Chennai, Tamil Nadu. The Company earlier executed 
definitive documents with CleanMax Enviro Energy 
Solutions Private Limited (CleanMax) for setting up 
captive power generating facility in the state of Haryana 
and Maharashtra having solar capacity of 14.85 MWp and 
4.00 MWp respectively.
(iii)	 The capital investment on energy conservation 
equipment’s;

invested on the following energy saving initiatives:

1.	
BRIEF OUTLINE ON THE CSR POLICY OF THE 
COMPANY
	
Sona BLW Precision Forgings Limited (“Company” or 
“Sona Comstar”) views Corporate Social Responsibility 
(“CSR”) not merely as a statutory obligation but as an 
integral part of its core philosophy and approach to 
long-term value creation. The Company recognises 
that its growth and progress are closely linked with 
the development and well-being of the communities in 
which it operates, and accordingly, seeks to generate 
meaningful impact through its CSR initiatives. In this 
context, the CSR Policy of the Company sets out an 
overarching framework, in accordance with applicable 
legal requirements, for undertaking CSR programs and 
initiatives as approved by the Board from time to time.
	
During the financial year under review, the Company, 
in line with its CSR Policy, continued to undertake 
focused interventions in the thematic areas of 
education, promotion of skill development and livelihood 
enhancement/ generation of employment, environmental 
sustainability, water conservation, eradicating hunger 
and women empowerment, with the objective of 
fostering inclusive development and strengthening 
community ecosystems. These initiatives are aligned 
with the Company’s commitment to responsible business 
conduct and its broader vision of contributing to societal 
progress alongside business growth.
	
The CSR Policy of the Company is structured around six 
thematic pillars, each representing one or more focused 
area of intervention aimed at contributing meaningfully 
to nation-building and social development:

PROVIDE THE EXECUTIVE SUMMARY ALONG 
WITH WEB-LINK(S) OF IMPACT ASSESSMENT 
OF CSR PROJECTS CARRIED OUT IN

APPLICABLE:
	
The CSR projects undertaken by the Company are in 
alignment with the activities prescribed under Schedule 
VII of the Companies Act, 2013, and demonstrate its 
sustained commitment towards generating meaningful 
impact. These projects were and shall continue to be 
implemented with an emphasis on delivering measurable 
outcomes, strengthening community capacity, and 
addressing identified social priorities in a structured 
manner. The CSR projects of the Company demonstrates 
alignment with multiple sustainable development goals 
(SDGs). Your Company has voluntarily commissioned 
InGovern Research Services Private Limited and 
Bluesky Sustainable Business LLP (hereinafter referred 
to as ‘Impact Assessor’) to carry out a Third-Party Impact 
Assessment for the CSR projects undertaken in Financial 
Year 2025-26.
	
A total of 11 (Eleven) CSR projects of the Company 
were tested for impact assessment. The projects were 
evaluated based on two key parameters (1) process 
maturity (design, governance and implementation quality) 
and (2) achievement of intended objectives (outputs, 
outcomes and early impact). The assessment was carried 
out using PMGA framework which is aligned with SEBI 
Social Auditing Standards and ISO 26000: Guidance on 
Social Responsibility. The evaluation method for impact 
assessment included desk reviews, a logic model approach, 
key informant interviews, focus group discussions, and 
mixed-method data analysis. The CSR projects were rated 
using BlueSky's proprietary scoring method. The Impact 
Assessment Report on CSR for Financial Year 2025-26, is 
attached along with this CSR Report.
	
The CSR project portfolio of the Company was rated 
as ‘Exemplary’ by the Impact Assessor. As noted in 
the Impact Assessment Report, Sona Comstar’s CSR 
portfolio reflects a mature and strategically diversified 
approach, combining direct community interventions 
with ecosystem-level investments. Further, the Impact 
Assessment Report also noted the strategic, system-
oriented impact creation by the CSR strategy of the 
Company.
	
As per MCA General Circular No. 14/2021 dated 
25th August, 2021, on FAQs on CSR, it is clarified that 
web-link to access the complete impact assessment 
reports and providing executive summary of the impact 
assessment reports in the annual report on CSR, shall be 
considered as sufficient compliance of Rule 8(3)(b) of the 
Companies (CSR Policy) Rules, 2014.

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
This report presents an independent CSR Impact Assessment 
of Sona BLW Precision Forgings Limited (“Sona Comstar”) 
portfolio of eleven flagship initiatives implemented during 
FY 2025-26.
Sona Comstar's CSR Portfolio has been rated as Exemplary 
using the Bluesky proprietary Process Maturity & Goals 
Achievement (PMGA) Framework borrows from the guidance 
available with Security and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) 
Social Auditing Standards and ISO 26000: Guidance on Social 
Responsibility.
Exemplary Projects have high process maturity and are 
successfully reaching their program goals. These projects 
represent best practices in implementation and impact, 
demonstrating an exemplary model for other projects 
to follow. Thematic areas of Sona Comstar's CSR portfolio 
are aligned with UN Sustainable Development Goals.
The assessment evaluates both:
•	 Process maturity (design, governance, implementation 
quality)
•	 Goal achievement (outputs, outcomes, early impact)
Sona Comstar’s CSR portfolio reflects a mature and 
strategically diversified approach, combining direct 
community interventions with ecosystem-level investments. 
At one end of the spectrum, programs such as sanitation, 
school infrastructure deliver immediate, large-scale human 
impact, addressing fundamental needs of nutrition, health, 
and dignity. At the other end, innovation-led initiatives such 
as FITT and IIMA Ventures operate upstream, shaping future 
industries, technological capabilities, and economic systems.
Between these, education and skilling programs act as 
bridges, translating opportunity into employability and long-
term socio-economic mobility. This layered portfolio design 
enables Sona Comstar to create impact across multiple time 
horizons:
•	 Immediate (health, sanitation)
•	 Medium-term (education, livelihoods)
•	 Long-term (innovation, environment)
Overall, the CSR strategy demonstrates a clear shift from 
compliance-driven philanthropy to strategic, systems-
oriented impact creation, with strong foundations for scale, 
sustainability and long-term value.

CHAPTER 1 | BACKGROUND
CSR Impact Assessment is a process which is individual to 
a company and depends on factors such as the size of the 
company and its maturity in addressing the expectations of 
the community it operates in. The purpose of the CSR Impact 
Assessment Report is for Sona Comstar to assess the impact of 
the activities of their CSR efforts, strive to maximise the impact 
of their CSR efforts and to identify areas for improvement and 
further engagement with the community.
About Sona Comstar
•	 Sona BLW Precision Forgings Limited (Sona Comstar) 
is one of the world's leading mobility technology 
companies, designing, manufacturing, and supplying 
highly engineered, mission-critical, high-quality, complex 
and bespoke systems and components for global mobility 
OEMs for both electrified and non-electrified powertrain 
segments.
Headquartered in Gurugram, India, Sona Comstar is a leading 
supplier for the fast-growing global EV market, with 35% of its 
automotive revenues from the EV market. It is also a global 
supplier with 12 manufacturing and assembly facilities across 
India, USA, Mexico, and China, and 49% of its sales are for 
international markets. The Company has a diversified revenue 
base across geographies, products, vehicle segments, and 
customers.
With a robust emphasis on research and development 
across its 5 R&D centres, Sona Comstar develops advanced 
drivetrain systems and high-performance motors for electric 
mobility, mmWave radar sensors and perception solutions for 
autonomous and connected applications, and safety-critical 
solutions for railway applications. It aims to be at the forefront 
of shaping the next generation of mobility technology and 
providing sustainable solutions for customer’s evolving needs. 
It aspires to become one of the world’s most respected and 
valuable mobility technology companies for its customers, 
employees and shareholders.
Sona Comstar CSR Policy
Sona Comstar’s CSR interventions consist of six pillars aimed 
to strengthen and reinforce the nation and the society:
1.	
Sona Comstar-Samridh Bharat (Environment and 
Sustainability)
2.	
Sona Comstar-Stree Shakti Bharat (Women 
Empowerment)
3.	
Sona Comstar-Swachh Bharat (Sanitation and Hygiene)
4.	
Sona Comstar-Saksham Bharat (Skill Development and 
Employability)
5.	
Sona Comstar-Swasth Bharat (Health and Nutrition)
6.	
Sona Comstar-Surakshit Bharat (Safety and Social 
Equity)
Sona Comstar has a well-defined and robust governance 
structure to oversee the implementation of the CSR Policy 
and monitoring of CSR projects as per the requirements of 
Section 135 of the Companies Act, - 2013. The CSR Policy of 
Sona Comstar can be accessed at
https://sonacomstar.com/files/policy/corporate-social-
responsibility-policy-policy-K6JBXy.pdf
Scope of CSR Impact Assessment Exercise
•	 To assess the CSR Projects performance as per the 
pre-defined targets defined in the MoU signed with 
Implementing Agency
•	 To assess if the objectives of the projects have been met.
CHAPTER 2 | IMPACT ASSESSMENT 
METHODOLOGY
Assessment Methodology and Strategic Focus
This CSR Impact Assessment evaluates eleven flagship CSR 
initiatives supported by Sona Comstar in FY 2025–26. The 
assessment was guided by Social Auditing Standards (SAS) 
under SEBI and ISO 26000:2018 on Social Responsibility. The 
PMGA framework (Process Maturity & Goals Achievement) 
was used to assess both implementation design and outcome 
achievement.
Evaluation methods included desk reviews, a logic model 
approach, key informant interviews (KIIs), focus group 
discussions (FGDs) and mixed-method data analysis. Projects 
were rated using BlueSky's proprietary scoring method 
to ensure accountability, transparency, and stakeholder-
centric impact. The projects were cumulatively rated as 
Exemplary, indicating high process maturity & significant goal 
achievements,
CSR Impact Assessment Methodology
The impact assessment methodology adheres to the Social 
Auditing Standards (SAS), regulated by the Institute of Social 
Auditors of India under SEBI, and aligns with ISO 26000:2018 
– Guidance on Social Responsibility. This structured approach 
ensures a comprehensive and transparent evaluation, 
incorporating the key methodologies outlined below.
In order to determine the impact of the CSR Projects supported 
by Sona Comstar, this impact assessment applied the PMGA 
framework (Process Maturity & Goals Achievement).
Based on the Theory of Change/ Logic Model and ISO 26000: 
Guidance for Social Responsibility, the Impact assessment of 
the CSR project is based on 2 sets of criterions.
1.	
Process Maturity Score: X Axis scored on 8 process 
criteria with a score range of Min 1- Max 5. It maps 
processes followed in implementing projects to the 
guidance framework available in ISO 26000 & NGRBC 
(principles of Social Responsibility).
2.	
Programme Goal Score: Y Axis scored on criterion of 
achieving the goals of the project agreed with the client 
with a score range of Min 1-Max 5. These criteria are 
specific to the project and maps to the guidance available 
in the Logic Model developed for the project.
EVALUATION FRAMEWORK
Maps the extent to which predefined project objectives as determined in the 
Project’s Logic Model were achieved and reported in terms of Key Metrics.

Desk Review
In-depth review of client documents including project progress reports, MOUs with implementing partners, 
project design, monitoring & evaluation systems. This step ensures assessing the process involved in program 
implementation to understand program efficiency.
Logic Model
Developed based on the desk review to map the relationship between inputs, activities, outputs, outcomes, and 
impacts, ensuring a structured evaluation approach.
Stakeholder Matrix
Identifies and categorises key stakeholders based on their influence, accountability, and interest, ensuring 
effective engagement in the assessment process.
Sampling
Qualitative Sample: Purposive sampling was applied for Focus Group Discussions (FGDs) and Key Informant 
Interviews (KIIs) to gather in-depth insights.
Assessment Tools
Key Informant Interviews (KIIs) and Focus Group Discussions (FGDs) to collect qualitative data.
Data Collection
A mixed-method approach to data collection combines quantitative data (measurable outcomes, statistics) with 
qualitative insights (beneficiary experiences, challenges), ensuring a comprehensive impact evaluation.
Data Analysis & 
Reporting
Findings were analysed on thematic coding for qualitative insights. The information so gathered is then 
triangulated to assess the impact of the initiative. 
Reporting
Structured reporting with key impact metrics and recommendations 
STAKEHOLDER MATRIX
A well designed stakeholder matrix ensures that not only 
are the contributors recognised, but the beneficiaries – 
those whose lives are impacted by the initiative are also 
accounted for. Including all relevant parties guarantees a 
comprehensive social impact assessment, reflective of the 
diverse perspectives at play within the initiative's ecosystem.
The 4 main types of stakeholders engaged for the social 
impact assessment were:
•	 Beneficiary Stakeholders – People who directly benefit 
from the project.
•	 Executing Stakeholders (Management) – Implementation 
Partner team who plan and manage the project.
•	 Executing Stakeholders (Field Teams) – Implementation 
Partner team who carry out the project activities on the 
ground.
•	 Oversight (Sponsor) – The organisation that supports, 
funds, and reviews the project.
The sampling approach ensured both breadth and depth in 
assessing the projects across various project locations.
Qualitative Feedback
A purposive sampling approach was used to gain in-
depth insights from key stakeholders, including school 
girls, trainers, school management representatives, and 
government officials. This mixed-method approach ensured 
a comprehensive evaluation of the project by integrating 
empirical data with stakeholder perspectives, strengthening 
the validity of the findings.

The IIT Delhi Innovation in Mobility Programme is a strategic 
CSR intervention anchored in enabling India’s transition 
toward:
•	 Low-carbon mobility systems
•	 Energy-efficient technologies
•	 Circular and clean manufacturing ecosystems
It represents a shift from direct beneficiary CSR models 
to ecosystem-level transformation, targeting upstream 
innovation in:
•	 Electric mobility systems
•	 Energy storage technologies
•	 Advanced materials
•	 Clean manufacturing
About Implementing Partner
The Foundation for Innovation and Technology Transfer 
(FITT), the industry interface organisation of the Indian 
Institute of Technology (IIT) Delhi, was established to foster 
the commercialisation of science and technology innovations. 
With a core mission to bridge the gap between academia and 
industry, FITT plays a crucial role in translating cutting-edge 
research into real-world applications by offering targeted 
support to startups, incubators, and technology-driven 
enterprises. Over the years, it has emerged as a key enabler 
of India’s innovation ecosystem, providing incubation support, 
mentorship, funding facilitation, and access to the intellectual 
and infrastructural resources of IIT Delhi.
Recognising the importance of innovation, the Government of 
India has introduced several policies, grants, and partnerships 
to encourage the development of Indian intellectual property 
and foster the growth of technology startups from concept 
to commercialisation. Despite this support, there remains 
a critical need for private sector involvement to provide 
sustained funding and mentorship for such innovations.
Aligned with this national vision, Sona Comstar, a strong 
proponent of intellectual property and innovation, has 
partnered with FITT, IIT Delhi to support startups in the safe, 
convenient, and eco-friendly mobility sector. This programme 
creates a collaborative platform that nurtures innovation 
and contributes meaningfully to India’s rapidly evolving 
technological landscape.
Project Overview
The programme has transitioned from early-stage R&D 
support in 2020 to ecosystem-building platform in 2025 
(including covid years). Over the years it has supported 
hardware-intensive startups, which typically face:
•	 Long gestation cycles
•	 High capital intensity
•	 High failure rates (80–90% at pre-seed stage) programme 
Description
The programme functions as a multi-dimensional innovation 
pipeline, combining:
•	 Capital infusion (grants)
•	 Technical incubation
•	 Market linkage
•	 Institutional credibility (IIT Delhi ecosystem)
Logic Model

•	
Industry mentoring from Sona Comstar
Activities
•	 National-level startup scouting
•	 Structured evaluation (tech + business + scalability)
•	 Milestone-based grant disbursement
•	 Continuous mentoring, quarterly reviews
•	 Industry validation and investor engagement

*Technology Readiness Levels (TRL) are a 9-level scale used to assess the 
maturity of a technology, ranging from initial research (TRL 1) to proven, 
market-ready deployment (TRL 9). Primarily developed by NASA, it helps 
organisations measure development progress and estimate feasibility 
for engineering projects, commonly used in sectors like engineering, 
manufacturing, and defense
Project Objectives
•	 Support the development and commercialisation of clean 
mobility technologies
•	 Enable deep-tech startups to move from prototype to 
market
•	 Strengthen indigenous R&D and manufacturing capabilities
The initiative specifically targets early-stage startups 
engaged in research, prototyping, and market validation in the 
e-mobility sector—a segment where startups often struggle 
due to limited access to capital, laboratories, and expert 
guidance. Through this partnership, the programme aims 
to empower these innovators and accelerate their journey 
toward impactful and scalable solutions.
Project Activities
The project followed a structured approach to identify and 
support high-potential startups across the country. It began 
with nationwide outreach through academic institutions, 
startup ecosystems, and digital campaigns, resulting in 900 
applications in FY 25 -26
A rigorous multi-stage evaluation process was implemented, 
including eligibility checks, technical screening, and expert 
panel reviews. The evaluation committee comprised IIT Delhi 
experts, industry leaders, investors, and legal professionals, 
ensuring a credible and comprehensive selection.
The programme provided grant-based financial support, 
with 80% of funds allocated directly to startups, while 
the remainder supported ecosystem development and 
programme operations at FIIT.
Selected startups benefited from incubation and technical 
support, including access to IIT labs, testing facilities, and 
research mentorship, enabling technology refinement and 
product development.
Finally, the initiative enabled market access and 
commercialisation through investor pitch events, industry 
pilots, and global exposure opportunities, helping startups 
scale and achieve market readiness.

Impact Assessment
The programme has led to significant behavioural and 
systemic shifts, as evidenced by key informant interviews 
(KIIs) and stakeholder insights. Notably, it has contributed to 
reduced risk aversion in deep-tech investments and fostered 
greater confidence in indigenous innovation. The initiative 
has also strengthened collaboration between startups 
and industry, while supporting the growth of the Make in 
India manufacturing ecosystem, creating a more enabling 
environment for innovation-led development.
In terms of cumulative impact, the programme has delivered 
results across multiple time horizons. In the short term, it 
has facilitated prototype development, advancement in 
Technology Readiness Levels (TRL), and the creation of 
intellectual property (IP). Moving into the medium term, 
startups have achieved market entry, begun generating 
revenue, and secured follow-on funding, indicating growing 
commercial viability.
Over the long term, the programme is expected to generate 
substantial economic, environmental, and systemic benefits. 
Economically, it is supporting the emergence of high-growth 
startups contributing to GDP and enabling the creation of 
innovation-driven industries.
Environmentally, it is accelerating decarbonisation efforts 
and reducing dependence on fossil fuels. Systemically, the 
initiative is helping to institutionalise a robust innovation 
ecosystem and reduce early-stage startup mortality, ensuring 
sustained impact and long-term resilience.
The programme demonstrates high economic multiplier 
effects, converting early-stage grants into large-scale private 
capital inflows.
Programme Stakeholders noted that:
“Milestone-based funding helps manage the high failure 
rates associated with early-stage startups by ensuring 
accountability and enabling steady progress.”
“Additionally, access to shared infrastructure significantly 
reduces capital barriers, allowing startups to innovate more 
efficiently without heavy upfront investments.”

About Implementing partner
IIMA Ventures, the innovation and entrepreneurship centre 
at the Indian Institute of Management Ahmedabad, is one

high-impact and technology-driven enterprises. Established 
to nurture entrepreneurship and innovation, the organisation 
provides early-stage startups with incubation support, seed 
funding, mentoring, market access, and strategic partnerships 
across sectors such as mobility, climate-tech, healthcare, and 
advanced manufacturing.
Recognising the importance of indigenous innovation 
and deep-tech entrepreneurship in driving sustainable 
economic growth, Sona Comstar partnered with CIIE-IIMA 
Ventures to support startups working in clean mobility, 
advanced manufacturing, and future technologies. Through

innovation-led economy aligned with national priorities such

PROJECT 3 | SONACOMSTAR – TATA STRIVE SKILL 
DEVELOPMENT CENTRE, CHENNAI

Name of Implementing Partner: Tata Strive

About Implementing partner
Tata STRIVE, an initiative of the Tata Community Initiatives 
Trust, is a national skill development programme established 
to bridge the gap between education and employability among 
Indian youth. The organisation focuses on delivering industry-
aligned vocational training, behavioural development, and 
placement-linked skilling programmes across sectors such 
as manufacturing, BFSI, hospitality, automotive, and emerging 
technologies.
With a vision to create sustainable employment opportunities 
for underserved youth, particularly women and first-generation 
learners, Tata STRIVE combines technical training with soft 
skills, counselling, and placement facilitation. Sona Comstar’s 
partnership with Tata STRIVE supports the establishment 
and strengthening of skill development centres across 
Chennai, Pune, and Faridabad, enabling youth to access 
industry-relevant training and formal employment pathways. 
The collaboration contributes to enhancing employability, 
workforce participation, and long-term socio-economic 
mobility among aspiring youth.
Project Overview
The Tata STRIVE Skill Development Centre in Chennai 
represents a structured and outcome-oriented intervention 
under the CSR mandate of livelihood generation and skill 
development. Implemented by Tata STRIVE under the Tata 
Community Initiatives Trust, the programme addresses 
the persistent gap between education and employability 
among youth, particularly those from rural and underserved 
backgrounds.
The programme integrates industry-aligned training, 
behavioural development, and placement facilitation into 
a single cohesive model. At the time of assessment (March 
2026), it reflects a mature skilling ecosystem, with strong 
evidence of both scale and outcomes. A total of 623 students 
have completed and trained (FY 2023-2026) demonstrating 
significant outreach and operational capacity.

The program follows a sequenced, process-driven 
implementation model, beginning with mobilisation and 
ending with placement and post-placement tracking.
•	 Community-driven mobilisation (10–15 days) ensures 
outreach to rural and semi-urban youth
•	 Psychometric assessments align candidates with suitable 
career paths
•	 Multi-level counselling (students + parents) addresses 
socio-cultural barriers, especially for women
•	 Training integrates technical instruction with behavioural 
development through interactive methods
•	 Curriculum is co-developed with industry and aligned with 
QP’S AS PER NSQF standards
•	 Assessments through TCS iON (third-party platform) 
enhance credibility
•	 A dedicated placement ecosystem connects students to 
employers

•	 426 students trained in FY 2025–26 (till March 2026)

significant shift toward gender inclusion, particularly in

A defining feature of the program is the hostel facility, 
which enables participation from rural and distant regions,

Post-Placement Tracking & Social Return on Investment 
(SROI)
Tata STRIVE tracks the employment outcomes of all certified 
youth for a period of one year from the date of certification. 
Connect attempts are made at 3, 6, 9, and 12 month intervals 
by the Tata STRIVE call centre team. In practice, the team is 
able to reach 30–40% of the total certified youth at any given 
interval — not for lack of effort, but due to factors outside the 
programme's control: changed phone numbers, workplace 
policies restricting phone use during working hours, travel, 
and similar reasons.
For the batch certified in 2024–25, the team made 227 
outreach instances (non-exclusive), of which 131 youth 
confirmed they were employed — roughly 60% of those 
reached. Extrapolating this to the full cohort, approximately 
120 youth can be considered employed. At an average 
monthly salary of INR 18,500, this translates to an annual 
income generation of INR 2.66 Cr (120 × 12 × 18,500).
For 2025–26, outreach was made across 313 instances (non-
exclusive), with 193 youth reporting active employment — a 
working rate of approximately 62%, at an average monthly 
salary of INR 18,000. On that basis, the income generated over 
6 months stands at INR 2.85 Cr (264 × 6 × 18,000).
Impact Assessment
At the current stage, the Tata STRIVE Skill Development 
Centre demonstrates strong outcome-level impact across 
employability, workforce participation, and behavioural 
transformation among youth beneficiaries.
While long-term livelihood sustainability and career 
progression outcomes will evolve over time, the intervention 
has already generated measurable improvements in 
employment readiness and access to formal job opportunities.
The significant outcome to date is the successful transition of 
youth from education or unemployment into structured skilling 
and employment pathways.
This transformation has resulted in:
•	 Improved employability and workplace readiness
•	 Increased participation of women in vocational and 
technical training
•	 Enhanced confidence, communication, and professional 
behaviour among students
•	 Greater awareness of industry-oriented career 
opportunities
Stakeholder feedback highlights the hostel facility as a critical 
enabler for women’s participation, especially for students from 
rural and mobility-constrained backgrounds.
STAKEHOLDER INSIGHTS
Centre Manager
•	 Strong performance in placements and gender inclusion
•	 Key challenge: mobilising women for technical roles
Trainer
•	 Major gap at entry: communication skills
•	 Strong post-training improvement
•	 Continuous tracking for one year
Students
•	 High satisfaction with curriculum and trainers
•	 Initial hostel adjustment challenges
•	 Strong confidence gains
FGDs
•	 Training is “interactive and different from college”
•	 Confidence and independence are key outcomes
•	 Travel and social barriers remain challenges

PROJECT 4 | PUNE – TATA STRIVE SKILL CENTRE 
(MAHARASHTRA)
Pune, as a leading automotive, manufacturing, and emerging 
technology hub, represents a high-potential ecosystem for 
advanced skill development aligned with Industry 4.0 trends. 
The region is characterised by strong demand for skilled 
professionals in automotive manufacturing, CNC machining, 
mechatronics, and increasingly in EV, automation, and digital 
services. Unlike Faridabad, Pune reflects relatively higher 
alignment between youth aspirations and industry demand, 
particularly in modern, technology-driven roles. However, 
gaps persist in terms of practical, job-ready skills and industry 
exposure, especially among first-time entrants into the 
workforce.
The Tata STRIVE model in Pune is therefore positioned to 
strengthen this ecosystem by delivering industry-aligned, 
future-ready training programs, combining technical skills with 
behavioural and workplace readiness modules. With its strong 
industry linkages and placement-driven approach, the centre 
can act as a catalyst for creating a skilled workforce that meets 
both current industrial needs and emerging technological 
transitions, particularly in EVs, automation, and advanced 
manufacturing.
The centre is ready infrastructurally and will resume the 
training activities soon
PROJECT 5 | FARIDABAD – TATA STRIVE SKILL 
CENTRE (HARYANA)
Faridabad, a major industrial hub in the NCR region, with 
over 6,000 MSMEs and 250+ large industries, presents a 
strong demand for skilled manpower across manufacturing, 
automotive, and electrical sectors.
The assessment highlights a critical paradox: while industries 
face acute skill shortages in roles such as CNC operators, 
electricians, welders, and assembly technicians, local 
youth demonstrate low aspiration for physically intensive 
manufacturing jobs, preferring sectors such as automotive 
(especially EV), BFSI, and digital services.
This demand–aspiration mismatch has led to a heavy 
dependence on migrant labour from neighbouring states. 
Additionally, existing infrastructure such as Government 
ITIs remains underutilised, with only 50–55% enrolment, 
indicating a disconnect between traditional skilling models 
and youth expectations.
In this context, the proposed Tata STRIVE centre is strategically 
positioned to bridge both the industry demand–supply gap 
and aspiration mismatch by offering short-term, job-oriented, 
apprenticeship-integrated courses in CNC, electrician, and 
EV technologies.
This is further strengthened by the inclusion of service-sector 
programs and hostel facilities, which will enhance accessibility, 
mobility, and retention, particularly for underserved youth.
The centre is ready infrastructurally and will resume the 
training activities soon.
PROJECT 6 | SUPPORTING BASIC EDUCATION IN 
PARTNERSHIP WITH VIDYA (VIDYA INTEGRATED 
DEVELOPMENT FOR YOUTH AND ADULTS)
CSR Pillar: Saksham Bharat programme
Name of Implementing Partner: Vidya Integrated

Project Location: Gurugram
About the Implementing Partner
Vidya Integrated Development for Youth and Adults (VIDYA) 
is a not-for-profit organisation dedicated to empowering 
underserved children, youth, and women through education 
and skill development initiatives. Established with a vision 
to enable equitable access to learning opportunities, VIDYA 
focuses on bridging educational gaps and supporting 
individuals to achieve their full potential. The organisation 
works across multiple programs that combine academic 
support, life skills, and vocational training, fostering holistic 
development and long-term self-reliance.
Project Overview
Vidya implemented 2 projects :
1.	
The National Institute of Open Schooling (NIOS) is 
India’s largest open and distance learning institution, 
established to provide flexible and inclusive educational 
opportunities for learners who are unable to continue 
formal schooling due to social, economic, or personal 
constraints. NIOS conducts public examinations twice 
annually, typically in April–May and October–November, 
while also offering an On-Demand Examination System 
for eligible learners.
	
The intervention covered 100 learners across Open 
Basic Education (OBE), Class 10, and Class 12 levels 
during the November 2025 to April 2026 period. 
The program combined classroom instruction with 
digital literacy, life-skills education, exposure visits, 
and entrepreneurship orientation to strengthen both 
academic and employability outcomes. Delivered 
through a team of five teachers along with program 
support staff, the initiative focused on enabling learners 
to complete formal certification while improving literacy, 
numeracy, confidence, and future livelihood prospects.
2.	
The Remedial Support programme at Vidya School, 
Gurugram, was designed to address learning gaps 
among 660 students from Grades 6 to 12 through 
targeted academic support and personalised instruction. 
Implemented between November 2025 and February 
2026, the intervention provided subject-specific remedial 
classes across core disciplines including Mathematics, 
Science, English, Accounts, and Economics through a 
team of over 20 teachers. The program adopted small-
group teaching methods, regular assessments, exam 
preparation support, and teacher-parent feedback 
mechanisms to strengthen academic performance, 
classroom participation, confidence, and study skills. The 
intervention also aimed to reduce the risk of academic 
underperformance and school dropouts by providing 
focused and continuous learning support aligned with 
mainstream school curriculum requirements.
Project Objectives
The project operates at two interconnected levels:
Student-Level Objectives:
•	 Strengthen foundational competencies in Math, Science, 
and English
•	 Reduce dropout risk and improve continuity
•	 Facilitate re-entry through NIOS pathways
•	 Build confidence, participation, and disciplined study habits
System-Level Objectives:
•	 Reduce reliance on unregulated private tuition ecosystems
•	 Reinforce trust in formal schooling systems
•	 Enable peer-driven, self-sustaining learning environments
This reflects a shift from short-term academic correction to 
long-term behavioural and institutional transformation.
Project Activities
The interventions follows a multi-layered, integrated model 
combining academic, behavioural, and ecosystem-level 
strategies:
•	 The academic layer delivers targeted remedial instruction 
based on continuous diagnostic assessments, ensuring 
that teaching is adaptive rather than standardised.
•	 The monitoring layer tracks attendance daily using an 
early-warning system. A key operational insight is the “2-
day absence rule,” which triggers immediate intervention. 
Approximately 80% of attendance disruptions are 
resolved at this early stage, largely through direct parent 
engagement.
•	 The engagement layer strengthens the ecosystem around 
the student, with regular teacher-parent communication 
and home visits for high-risk cases, ensuring that barriers 
outside the classroom are addressed.
•	 The assessment layer introduces rigor through topic-wise 
quizzes and mock exams, enabling differentiation between 
conceptual and analytical gaps - an important distinction 
often missed in traditional systems.
•	 The pathway layer integrates NIOS as a formal mechanism 
for certification and re-entry, ensuring that students who 
have disengaged are not permanently excluded from the 
education system.
This architecture ensures adaptive learning, strong monitoring, 
and systemic student support.

Project Outputs
The two program’s activities including diagnostic testing, 
remedial sessions, continuous assessments, attendance 
tracking, and parental engagement translate into strong and 
measurable outputs.
In addition to examination preparation and academic support, 
the NIOS programme integrated digital literacy, spoken 
English, career guidance, life-skills training, health and 
wellness awareness, and counselling to strengthen learner 
confidence and future readiness. The intervention also 
included workshops, exposure activities, and continuous 
mentoring to support students in completing formal education 
and progressing towards higher education, vocational training, 
entrepreneurship, or employment.
Impact Assessment
The impact of both the Vidya Project is best understood 
through its causal pathways, validated through stakeholder 
interactions (KIIs and FGDs).
•	 targeted remediation leads to conceptual clarity. As 
students begin to understand core subjects, their fear 
reduces, test scores improve, and confidence increases.
•	 Improved teaching quality and engaging pedagogy drive 
intrinsic motivation, reflected in sustained attendance and 
negligible dropout levels.
•	 Continuous assessment and teacher support foster 
disciplined study habits, with students reportedly studying 
for extended hours often exceeding six hours per day.
•	 At the household level, reduced dependence on private 
tuition lowers financial burden and increases parental trust 
in the school system, strengthening educational continuity.
Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) For Vidya Remedial 
Support programme

Pre-Board II for Grade 10 (2025–26)

assessment scores: Approximately 8–12% improvement 
in academic performance across core subjects
•	 Percentage of students showing improvement in English 
reading and writing skills: 66%
•	 Percentage of students showing improvement in 
Mathematics competency: 75%
•	 Percentage of students progressing from below-grade 
level to grade-appropriate competency: 65%
•	 Improvement in average student attendance: 19%
•	 Reduction in absenteeism/dropout rate: 87%
Learning has shifted from fear-driven to confidence-driven, 
and classrooms have evolved from passive environments 
to spaces of active participation. Students exhibit increased 
agency, taking ownership of their learning, while families 
report reduced conflict around education and greater trust 
in schools.
The project also creates a cost substitution effect, reducing 
reliance on private tuition - an important relief for low - income 
households. Over time, improved educational continuity 
enhances employability prospects, contributing to upward 
economic mobility.
At a structural level, the intervention addresses systemic gaps 
in public education delivery by:
•	 Replacing fragmented tuition ecosystems
•	 Reinforcing schools as primary learning institutions
•	 Enabling peer-led learning cultures
This systemic correction represents one of the program’s most 
critical contributions.

PROJECT 7 | BUILDING OF INFRASTRUCTURE IN 
SCHOOLS IN NUH, HARYANA
CSR Pillar: Saksham Bharat Programme
Name of Implementing Partner: Hindu Shikshah Foundation
Project Locations: Manesar, Gurugram
About Implementing Partner
The intervention is anchored in Quality Education, with strong 
linkages to Student Health & Hygiene and Clean Energy 
Access. By addressing foundational infrastructure gaps, the 
project adopts an integrated CSR approach that enables both 
learning outcomes and well-being improvements.
The project has been implemented across two schools in Nuh 
district:
•	 Hindu Vidya Niketan - 860 students (516 boys and 344 
girls)
•	 Hindi Senior Secondary School -790 students (553 boys 
& 235 girls)
Nuh (Mewat) is widely recognised as an educationally 
and socio-economically backward district in Haryana, 
characterised by low literacy levels, gender disparities in 
education, and weak public infrastructure. The region also 
faces chronic infrastructure deficits, including unreliable 
electricity and limited access to safe drinking water, which 
directly impact schooling outcomes.
The intervention was completed in February 2026, with 
approximately one month elapsed since implementation. 
Therefore, this assessment focuses on validated outputs and 
early adoption signals, rather than long-term impact.
Scale of Intervention
•	 2 schools covered
•	 2 RO water systems installed (one per school)
•	 Solar-powered electricity system installed Hindu Vidya 
Niketan
•	 Science laboratories established (Physics, Chemistry, 
Biology) at Hindu Vidya Niketan
•	 UPS and LAN systems provisioned to support digital and 
uninterrupted operations at Hindu Vidya Niketan
•	 Maintenance systems established (2-year AMC + assigned 
personnel)
Project Overview
The school infrastructure intervention in Nuh, Haryana is 
a comprehensive education-support initiative aimed at 
improving foundational learning environments in underserved 
schools. Implemented in partnership with Hindu Shiksha 
Foundation, the project addresses critical infrastructure gaps 
related to safe drinking water, electricity, science education, 
and digital enablement.
Situated in Nuh, one of Haryana’s most educationally 
vulnerable regions, the project seeks to create enabling 
conditions for improved student participation, comfort, and 
academic engagement. By strengthening essential school 
infrastructure, the initiative contributes to enhancing the 
quality of education and building more resilient and student-
friendly learning ecosystems.
Project Objectives
The intervention was designed to address critical bottlenecks 
affecting the learning environment, with the following 
objectives:
•	 Provide safe and reliable drinking water within school 
premises
•	 Ensure continuous electricity supply for classrooms and 
facilities

A critical strength is that outputs are not just installed but 
fully integrated into daily school functioning, supported by 
maintenance systems and institutional ownership.
Impact Assessment
At present, the intervention demonstrates strong performance 
at the output level and early adoption stage:

water sources; improved hygiene practices

academic engagement

This intervention represents a strong foundation-stage
